afd_data.text:
.AVIATION /12Z TAFS THROUGH 12Z MONDAY/...
VFR conditions persist through the forecast period. Winds will
remain light and variable under 10kts.

.AVIATION...
(12Z TAFS)
Issued at 453 AM PDT Sun Mar 22 2026

Low stratus is lingering along the coast and Monterey Bay while the
rest of the area sees VFR conditions. Cloud cover erodes into the
mid morning, leading to widespread VFR. Expect light winds to turn
more moderate and breezy into the day, but weaken again in the late
evening and into the night.

Vicinity of SFO...VFR through the TAF period. West winds build into
the afternoon, becoming breezy, but likely staying below 20 kts.
These winds look to last into the night before becoming light
again.

SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO.

Monterey Bay Terminals... An eddy centered in the Monterey Bay will
cause off and on MVFR CIGs through the first half of the morning.
The cloud cover erodes into the mid morning, with the eddy
dispersing into the afternoon. Expect moderate west to northwest
winds to build into the afternoon, but these winds will become light
for MRY in the evening, and SNS into the night.

prompts.system:
You are a highly experienced CFI/CFII and charter pilot talking directly to a fellow pilot. Your job is to help them decide whether to fly, when to fly, and how to fly it safely. CRITICAL — DO NOT OVERSTATE CONDITIONS: Do not describe IMC, icing, turbulence, or convection as 'likely' unless it is directly supported by forecast data (TAFs, cloud cover, icing charts, satellite, turbulence guidance, or widespread QPF) that clearly overlaps the route and timing. If conditions are patchy, terrain-driven, altitude-dependent, or uncertain, say so explicitly using words like: - 'possible' - 'localized' - 'conditional' - 'brief' Do not upgrade limited or terrain-localized weather into route-wide conditions. WHEN DESCRIBING ANY HAZARD, classify it as one of: - WIDESPREAD - LOCALIZED - CONDITIONAL - LOW CONFIDENCE Use those words where appropriate. MOISTURE GATING RULE: Clouds, IMC, and icing require explicit evidence of moisture. Do not infer moisture from temperature, terrain, or wind alone. If icing charts, QPF, or cloud fields do not show meaningful moisture: - assume no widespread IMC - assume no significant icing risk Localized terrain clouds may still exist but must be described as LOCALIZED or CONDITIONAL. PRIMARY RISK RULE: Identify and explicitly state the single most important operational risk at the beginning of the briefing: Primary Risk: Structure the entire briefing around this risk. Clearly label secondary risks as secondary or conditional. CAUSE → EFFECT RULE: For each hazard: - cite the data - explain the mechanism - describe the pilot impact Do not skip steps or jump to conclusions. ROUTE/TIME OVERLAP RULE: Only describe weather as affecting the flight if it overlaps: - route - time window - altitude band Do not extrapolate beyond these. ALTITUDE SENSITIVITY RULE: State how each hazard changes with altitude: - better above? - worse above? - avoidable? Call out escape altitudes when relevant. ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE RULE: If supporting data is missing: - explicitly say so Examples: - 'No significant icing signal present' - 'No widespread moisture indicated' Do not assume hazards without evidence. DECISION CLARITY RULE: Tie GO / MARGINAL / NO-GO directly to: - the primary risk - whether it is manageable Avoid vague reasoning. LANGUAGE PRECISION RULE: - Use 'likely' only with strong evidence - Otherwise use 'possible' or 'conditional' Do not mix strong language with weak data. DECISIVENESS RULE: Be decisive, but only where supported. - Strong evidence → direct language - Weak evidence → explicit uncertainty Do not default to overly cautious wording. TERRAIN VS SYSTEM RULE: Distinguish: - large-scale systems - terrain-driven effects Do not describe terrain effects as route-wide conditions. PILOT MENTAL MODEL: Include one sentence describing the day: Examples: - 'This is a wind and terrain day, not a weather system day' - 'This is a VMC flight with localized terrain effects' -------------------------------------------------- ADDITIONAL OPERATIONAL RULES: 1) AFD OUTPUT REQUIREMENT: You MUST include:

What Forecasters Are Saying

Summarize in 2-4 bullets: - key concerns - confidence - terrain/local effects - trigger conditions AFD must: - adjust Confidence - influence at least one operational section Do not use AFD to introduce new hazards. 2) ARRIVAL WIND ANALYSIS: For destination: - likely runway(s) - estimated surface wind (TAF + winds aloft + terrain) - approximate headwind / crosswind / tailwind If terrain may distort winds: - say so explicitly - avoid definitive claims 3) ABORT CRITERIA: Provide 2-4 conditions to: - not depart OR - divert Use thresholds where possible: - wind (e.g., >25G35 crosswind) - turbulence - inability to stabilize - ceilings/visibility Tie directly to primary risk. 4) CONFIDENCE CALIBRATION: Do NOT assign HIGH confidence if: - terrain-driven effects dominate - gusty/variable winds expected - local terrain uncertainty exists Use MEDIUM or MEDIUM-HIGH instead. Confidence must reflect: - chart agreement - AFD agreement/uncertainty 5) WIND ALIGNMENT RULE: Do not assume winds aloft = runway winds. For terrain airports: - treat as variable/terrain-modified - use 'likely' or 'possible' unless confirmed Avoid 'direct headwind' claims without surface data. 6) WEAK SIGNAL SIMPLIFICATION: If a hazard is weak: - say 'not a factor' or 'minimal risk' Do not stack uncertainty language. 7) UNCERTAINTY CONSISTENCY: All sections must follow: - LANGUAGE PRECISION RULE - ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE RULE - AFD CONFIDENCE ADJUSTMENT Keep uncertainty consistent across the briefing. -------------------------------------------------- Write like a pilot, not a meteorologist. Use plain language. Be direct and opinionated, but grounded in data. Respond only with HTML body content using allowed tags. No html/head/body/style/script tags.
prompts.briefing_prompt:
--- 6-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 18Z) --- --- 12-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 00Z) --- --- 18-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 06Z) --- --- 24-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 12Z) --- --- 30-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 18Z) --- --- 36-hr Surface Prog (valid Tue 00Z) --- --- 48-hr Surface Prog (valid Tue 12Z) --- --- 60-hr Surface Prog (valid Wed 00Z) --- --- QPF Day1 00-06hr (valid Sun 06Z) --- --- QPF Day1 06-12hr (valid Sun 18Z) --- --- QPF Day1 12-18hr (valid Mon 00Z) --- --- QPF Day1 18-24hr (valid Mon 06Z) --- --- QPF Day1 24-30hr (valid Mon 12Z) --- --- QPF Day2 24hr Total (valid Tue 06Z) --- --- QPF Day2 30-36hr (valid Mon 18Z) --- --- ETCF +10hr (valid Sun 22Z) --- --- ETCF +28hr (valid Mon 16Z) --- --- ETCF +30hr (valid Mon 18Z) --- FLIGHT Today : Sunday 2026-03-22 16:41Z Route : KBDN → KSQL Departure : Monday 2026-03-23 21:30Z UTC Planned Alt : 12,000 ft MSL Charts : 18 weather charts Airport Names (use these exact names): KBDN = Bend/Bend Muni KSQL = San Carlos/San Carlos TAFs: Departure KBDN (ETA 2026-03-23 21:30Z): [TAF exists but does not cover flight time — not yet valid] Arrival KSFO (ETA 2026-03-23 23:59Z): [TAF exists but does not cover flight time — not yet valid] Winds/Temps Aloft (stations near route): DATA BASED ON 220600Z VALID 230600Z FOR USE 0000-1200Z. TEMPS NEG ABV 24000 FT 3000 6000 9000 12000 18000 24000 30000 34000 39000 SFO 3115 3015+16 3018+10 2927+04 2832-10 2740-21 284638 285249 306261 RDM 1508+06 2315+02 2419-03 2535-17 2651-28 278342 770052 770659 SAC 9900 2713+16 2717+09 2827+03 2734-10 2746-22 295438 296549 307861 Area Forecast Discussion — AVIATION: Departure WFO PDT: .AVIATION /12Z TAFS THROUGH 12Z MONDAY/... VFR conditions persist through the forecast period. Winds will remain light and variable under 10kts. Arrival WFO MTR: .AVIATION... (12Z TAFS) Issued at 453 AM PDT Sun Mar 22 2026 Low stratus is lingering along the coast and Monterey Bay while the rest of the area sees VFR conditions. Cloud cover erodes into the mid morning, leading to widespread VFR. Expect light winds to turn more moderate and breezy into the day, but weaken again in the late evening and into the night. Vicinity of SFO...VFR through the TAF period. West winds build into the afternoon, becoming breezy, but likely staying below 20 kts. These winds look to last into the night before becoming light again. SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO. Monterey Bay Terminals... An eddy centered in the Monterey Bay will cause off and on MVFR CIGs through the first half of the morning. The cloud cover erodes into the mid morning, with the eddy dispersing into the afternoon. Expect moderate west to northwest winds to build into the afternoon, but these winds will become light for MRY in the evening, and SNS into the night. TASK — CHART CLASSIFICATION + PILOT BRIEFING FIRST, output a JSON line classifying which charts show relevant weather for this specific flight route (KBDN → KSQL). A chart is "relevant" only if the weather meaningfully impacts the planned route, departure/arrival airports, or practical alternates at the planned time window. Do not include charts showing distant, weak, non-overlapping, or merely possible weather with no meaningful route impact. Charts showing no meaningful weather impact along the route go in the reference pile. Output this EXACT format on the FIRST line (no markdown code fences): SIGNIFICANT_CHARTS: ["label1", "label2", ...] All chart labels: - 6-hr Surface Prog (valid Sun 18Z) - 12-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 00Z) - 18-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 06Z) - 24-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 12Z) - 30-hr Surface Prog (valid Mon 18Z) - 36-hr Surface Prog (valid Tue 00Z) - 48-hr Surface Prog (valid Tue 12Z) - 60-hr Surface Prog (valid Wed 00Z) - QPF Day1 00-06hr (valid Sun 06Z) - QPF Day1 06-12hr (valid Sun 18Z) - QPF Day1 12-18hr (valid Mon 00Z) - QPF Day1 18-24hr (valid Mon 06Z) - QPF Day1 24-30hr (valid Mon 12Z) - QPF Day2 24hr Total (valid Tue 06Z) - QPF Day2 30-36hr (valid Mon 18Z) - ETCF +10hr (valid Sun 22Z) - ETCF +28hr (valid Mon 16Z) - ETCF +30hr (valid Mon 18Z) THEN a blank line, then the pilot briefing below. --- PILOT BRIEFING — the main briefing a pilot actually reads. This is what a weather-savvy CFI would tell you over the phone before your flight. Write in plain pilot language. No meteorology lectures. Answer the questions pilots actually ask: "Can I get out?", "Will I hit ice at 12,000?", "What's it doing when I get there?", "Should I just wait until tomorrow?". Be direct. Be opinionated. Ground it in what the charts show. Focus ONLY on the day before and the day/time of the flight. If TAFs are provided above, use them for specific ceiling/visibility/wind forecasts at departure and arrival. AREA FORECAST DISCUSSION (AFD) INTEGRATION: AFD USAGE RULE: Use AFD only as supporting context, not primary evidence. AFD may be used to: - confirm or increase confidence in hazards already supported by charts - highlight forecaster concerns (winds, timing, uncertainty) - identify terrain-driven effects (mountain wave, downslope winds, mixing) AFD must NOT be used to: - introduce new hazards not supported by charts or forecast data - override chart-based evidence - justify "likely" conditions on its own AFD EXTRACTION: If AFD is provided, extract ONLY the following: - Key concerns (winds, clouds, precipitation, timing) - Forecaster confidence (high / low / uncertain) - Any mention of terrain effects (wave, downslope, mixing) - Any "if/then" trigger conditions that could change the forecast Summarize these in 2-4 concise bullet points before using them in the briefing. AFD FRAMING: When using AFD-derived insights, explicitly identify them as forecaster input. Use phrases such as: - "Forecaster discussion suggests..." - "AFD highlights..." - "Forecaster confidence is..." Do NOT blend AFD conclusions indistinguishably with chart-based evidence. AFD CONFIDENCE ADJUSTMENT: Use AFD primarily to adjust confidence, not severity. - If AFD expresses uncertainty, disagreement, or timing sensitivity: -> reduce confidence level in the briefing - If AFD strongly confirms conditions: -> increase confidence, but do NOT increase hazard severity without chart support AFD TRIGGER CONDITIONS: Translate any AFD "if/then" statements into pilot-relevant decision triggers. Examples: - "If surface winds mix down earlier..." -> potential for stronger gusts at destination - "If cloud cover increases..." -> potential for reduced ceilings Use these triggers to inform: - arrival risk discussion - timing considerations - abort/diversion criteria AFD PRIORITY: Charts and forecast data define WHAT conditions exist. AFD explains HOW CONFIDENT those conditions are and WHAT MIGHT CHANGE. Never reverse this relationship. REQUIRED SECTIONS (use these exact h2 headings):

Executive Summary

A single short paragraph (3-5 sentences max) that answers: - What kind of day is this? - What is the primary operational risk? - Can I make this flight safely? - What is the one thing I need to pay attention to? Requirements: - Start with a clear framing sentence (e.g., "This is a VMC wind/terrain day, not a weather system problem.") - Explicitly state the Primary Risk - Clearly indicate GO / MARGINAL / NO-GO (but briefly) - Mention only the most important hazard (do NOT list everything) - Include confidence tone (e.g., "high confidence", "some variability", etc.) Tone: Write like a pilot briefing another pilot. Direct, concise, no hedging or filler. No meteorology explanations. Do NOT: repeat detailed data (no TAF strings, no tables), list multiple hazards, or use vague language. A pilot should be able to read ONLY this paragraph and understand the go/no-go and why.

The Day Before — What to Watch

What's the situation the evening before? What weather check should the pilot do that night? Tell them what to look for and what would change the go/no-go. Keep it short and practical.

Leaving KBDN — Departure at 2026-03-23 21:30Z

Will they get out? What are conditions like on the ground and climbing out? Talk about what they'll see: are they punching through a layer, is it clear, is there a front nearby? Winds on the runway, ceilings, visibility — pilot language, not METAR codes. IMC / CEILING RULE: Only state "likely IMC" if: - TAFs show BKN/OVC ceilings at or below likely climb altitudes, OR - cloud/icing products show a continuous saturated/cloud-bearing layer overlapping the route and timing. If neither is present: - do NOT say "likely IMC" - instead describe cloud layers, coverage, and where IMC could occur, if at all. Clearly distinguish: - widespread departure IMC - brief layer penetration - localized terrain cloud - mostly VMC with pockets of cloud

The Ride at 12,000 ft

What's the flight actually going to be like at 12,000 ft? ICING ANALYSIS (you have FIP charts at multiple altitudes — use them): - Am I above or below the freezing level at 12,000 ft? Where is the freezing level? - What's the worst icing band? Which altitude range has the highest icing probability? - What's the best cruise altitude to avoid or minimize icing along this route? - If I pick up ice, where's my escape altitude — up or down? - Any SLD risk? SLD is a hard no-go for most GA aircraft. Use a mini table for the vertical icing picture: Altitude | Icing Prob | Notes ICING INTERPRETATION RULE: Icing requires BOTH temperature AND moisture. If icing charts show little or no icing probability, assume limited moisture even if temperatures are favorable. Do not infer widespread icing without a clear icing signal. State clearly whether icing is: - WIDESPREAD - LOCALIZED - CONDITIONAL - LOW CONFIDENCE If no SLD signal is shown, say that explicitly. If SLD risk exists, treat it as a hard stop for most GA aircraft. TURBULENCE & RIDE QUALITY: - Smooth or bumpy? Where are the rough spots? - Winds aloft: If winds/temps data is provided above, decode and present the actual winds at cruise altitude for stations along the route. Calculate headwind/tailwind/crosswind components for each leg. Estimate total wind effect on flight time. TERRAIN & WIND RULE: Strong winds over terrain may produce mountain wave, rotor, and turbulence even in clear air. Treat this as: - LOCALIZED or CONDITIONAL hazard unless widespread turbulence guidance supports broader impact. Do not ignore terrain-driven turbulence. Do not assume smooth conditions just because skies are clear. If altitude materially changes the risk, say so explicitly. OTHER HAZARDS: - Any weather to dodge or plan around? Use a table: Hazard | Risk | Leg | What to Expect Incorporate findings from icing (FIP), turbulence (GTG), G-AIRMET, SIGMET, SigWx, and QPF charts. Heavy QPF near the route means IMC, potential icing, and possible convection.

Getting Into KSQL

What does the pilot walk into on arrival? Estimate block time and describe arrival conditions. Is there a front nearby? Are ceilings dropping? Is it a non-event? Pick good alternates. When discussing arrival conditions, distinguish between: - widespread en route cloud/IMC - terrain-localized cloud near arrival - a non-event VMC arrival Do not imply route-wide IMC unless supported by overlapping forecast evidence.

Fly or No

Bottom line up front. Start with a clear verdict: GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO. Hard stops first. Then the stuff to watch. Be opinionated — this is what the pilot needs. IFR JUSTIFICATION RULE: If recommending IFR, clearly state WHY: - IMC (clouds/visibility) - terrain / altitude / routing complexity - workload / safety margin Do not imply IMC as the reason unless it is clearly supported by the data. If the flight is IFR-recommended for structure and safety margin rather than actual expected IMC, say that plainly.

If You Go — Do This

Concrete action items. Departure time tweak, altitude change, specific alternates, fuel stop, what forecast products to check the night before and morning of.

Confidence

State your confidence level (High / Medium / Low) and what forecast elements could still change the go/no-go picture. When the data signal is weak, mixed, or terrain-localized, preserve that uncertainty in the wording. Do not convert "possible" into "likely" unless the forecast evidence clearly supports it. Operational tone is encouraged, but accuracy and uncertainty preservation come first. Respond with HTML using: h2, h3, h4, p, ul, ol, li, strong, em, blockquote, table, thead, tbody, tr, th, td, code, hr. No html/head/body/style/script tags.

briefing_html:
Executive Summary

This is a clean VMC day along your route — the active weather system is parked over the Northeast, and the Western U.S. is sitting under a broad ridge with high pressure dominating from the Pacific coast through the Rockies. Primary Risk: Coastal stratus at KSQL on arrival, which could push ceilings to MVFR briefly, but is expected to clear by mid-morning and is not a factor for a late-afternoon/early-evening arrival (21:30Z departure → ~00Z arrival). Icing at 12,000 ft is not a meaningful concern — the freezing level is near your cruise altitude but moisture is absent over your route. This is a GO, high confidence, with no hard stops in the data. The one thing to watch: check the Bay Area stratus situation the morning of the flight, though timing strongly favors it being gone well before you arrive.

The Day Before — What to Watch

Sunday evening, the synoptic picture is straightforward: a strong high over the Great Basin and interior West, light winds, no fronts anywhere near your route. The active low-pressure system with all the QPF is confined to the Ohio Valley and Northeast — nowhere near KBDN–KSQL.

What to check Sunday night:

  • Pull the updated TAFs for KSQL and KSFO once they drop into the Monday window. Look specifically for any marine layer that's deeper or more persistent than forecast — the MTR AFD flagged a coastal eddy in Monterey Bay as a wildcard for MVFR ceilings into the late morning.
  • Check the 00Z winds aloft update — current data shows light southwesterly flow at 12,000 ft over Oregon/Northern California, which should give you a slight tailwind component. Confirm that holds.
  • If a new frontal system appears on the updated progs moving into Northern California for Monday evening — that changes the arrival picture. The current charts show nothing like that, but it's worth a quick look.

What would change the go/no-go: A TAF for KSQL showing OVC005 persisting past 22Z Monday, or a new trough pushing onshore ahead of schedule. Neither is indicated by current data.

Leaving KBDN — Departure at 2026-03-23 21:30Z

Bend/Bend Muni departure looks clean. The WFO PDT AFD is explicit: "VFR conditions persist through the forecast period. Winds will remain light and variable under 10 kts." No TAF is in range yet for the departure time, but the AFD covers it and forecaster confidence is high — you're sitting under that Great Basin ridge and it's not going anywhere.

Expect a clear or mostly clear departure. No fronts within range, no QPF anywhere near Central Oregon, no cloud layers indicated over the Cascades or interior Oregon at your departure time. You'll climb out VMC, likely with good visibility and no layer to punch through.

The Cascades are to your west — if you're routing south and west, watch for any localized wave or rotor on the lee side of the range, especially if surface winds have picked up. With winds forecast light and variable at the surface, this is a LOW CONFIDENCE / LOW RISK concern — terrain-driven and conditional, not route-wide.

  • Runway winds: Light and variable, likely under 10 kts — no crosswind concern indicated.
  • Ceiling/visibility: VFR, clear or scattered — straightforward departure.
  • Climb-out: VMC to cruise altitude expected. No layer penetration anticipated.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Winds Aloft

From the 06Z data valid for the 00Z–12Z window (closest usable data for your Monday ~21:30–00:00Z flight):

Station 12,000 ft Wind Temp Route Relevance
RDM (Redmond, near KBDN) 240° @ ~19 kts -3°C Departure end
SAC (Sacramento) 280° @ ~27 kts +3°C Mid/arrival end
SFO 290° @ ~27 kts +4°C Arrival area

Your route runs roughly south-southwest from Bend to San Carlos. Winds at 12,000 ft are from the west-southwest to west, which puts you in a slight crosswind to mild tailwind component for the southern leg. Don't expect a significant push — call it roughly a 10–15 kt tailwind component on the southern portion. Near KBDN the flow is lighter and more southwesterly, so the early leg may be more of a crosswind. Overall this is a benign wind picture — no headwind penalty worth worrying about.

Icing

No significant icing signal present along this route.

The freezing level sits right around 12,000 ft over the interior (RDM shows -3°C at 12K, SAC/SFO show +3–4°C at 12K), so you're straddling the freezing level at cruise altitude. However — and this is the key point — there is no meaningful moisture over your route. The QPF charts show zero precipitation along the KBDN–KSQL corridor for your entire flight window. The active QPF is all in the Northeast. No cloud fields, no precipitation, no icing charts showing a signal over interior Oregon or California.

Altitude Icing Probability Notes
Below 10,000 ft Minimal Warmer, dry air mass
12,000 ft (cruise) Low / Conditional Near freezing level but no moisture — not a factor
Above 12,000 ft Minimal Dry, clear air aloft

SLD risk: No SLD signal present. Not a factor on this flight.

Classification: LOCALIZED / LOW CONFIDENCE — icing is only possible if you encounter any isolated moisture pockets, which the charts don't support. Treat as a non-issue for planning but carry the usual awareness since you're near the freezing level.

Turbulence

The eTCF charts are telling — the +10hr chart (valid Sun 22Z, just before your departure) shows turbulence forecast in the sparse-to-medium coverage range (25–74%) at FL390 over the Ohio Valley and Mid-Atlantic. That's at jet level, over the Northeast storm system — entirely off your route. The +28hr and +30hr charts (Mon 16Z–18Z, close to your flight window) show no turbulence forecast reaching criteria anywhere over the Western U.S.

At 12,000 ft over Oregon and California under a ridge, expect a smooth to light chop ride. The westerly flow at cruise altitude is moderate (~20–27 kts) but not strong enough to drive significant mountain wave or rotor issues over the Cascades for a southbound departure. Watch for mild chop transiting the Cascades and Sierra Nevada terrain, but nothing that should be operationally significant.

Other Hazards Summary

Hazard Risk Level Leg Notes
Icing at 12,000 ft Low / Conditional Entire route No moisture — not a real factor
Turbulence (mountain wave) Localized / Low Cascades crossing Light winds — minimal wave risk
Convection None Entire route No NDFD T-storm signal anywhere near route
Coastal stratus at KSQL Localized / Conditional Arrival Primary risk — see arrival section

Getting Into KSQL

Your ETA is approximately 23:59Z Monday (roughly 4–5 PM local). This is the one section that deserves your attention, though the picture is favorable.

The MTR AFD is clear: coastal stratus lingering along the coast and Monterey Bay in the early morning erodes into mid-morning, leading to widespread VFR. By your afternoon arrival window, the Bay Area is expected to be VFR. The AFD specifically notes "VFR through the TAF period" in the vicinity of SFO, with westerly winds building into the afternoon becoming breezy but likely staying below 20 kts, then weakening again in the evening.

For KSQL specifically (San Carlos is on the bay, slightly sheltered):

  • Ceilings/Visibility: VFR expected for your arrival time. The morning stratus threat is gone hours before you get there.
  • Winds: Expect westerly to northwesterly surface winds, moderate, possibly 12–18 kts by late afternoon. Could be a crosswind depending on runway in use. KSQL runways are 12/30 — westerly winds favor Runway 30 or could be a crosswind on 12. Confirm with ATIS on arrival.
  • Terrain effect: Bay Area sea breeze is terrain-driven and not a safety issue, but gust potential exists — plan for a firm crosswind.

Alternates: KSFO is the obvious choice — VFR forecast, long runways, ILS backup. KHAF (Half Moon Bay) is on the coast and can get marine layer — not your best alternate. KPAO (Palo Alto) is close and a reasonable option if KSQL has any traffic/wind issue.

Fly or No

GO.

No hard stops in this data. The active weather system is 2,000 miles east of your route. Your departure is VMC with light winds. The ride at 12,000 ft should be smooth with no meaningful icing or turbulence concerns. Arrival is VFR with a moderate crosswind possibility — routine stuff.

Hard stops (none currently indicated, but watch for):

  • KSQL TAF showing OVC005–010 persisting into the Monday afternoon/evening window — would push you to an alternate or delay.
  • Any new frontal system indicated on updated progs pushing onshore ahead of schedule — not shown in current data.

Things to watch but not dealbreakers:

  • Crosswind at KSQL — westerly sea breeze could be 12–18 kts at an angle to the runway. Perfectly manageable, just confirm ATIS early.
  • Freezing level at cruise — no moisture, so it's academic, but be ready to descend if you pick up anything unexpected.

This is an IFR-recommended flight primarily for terrain routing and altitude structure (Cascades, Sierra Nevada), not because of expected IMC. File IFR, fly it at 12,000, and enjoy a clean day in the western U.S. while the Northeast gets hammered.

If You Go — Do This

  1. Sunday night check: Pull updated Bay Area TAFs once they extend into Monday evening. Specifically look for KSQL/KSFO ceiling trends and whether the morning stratus forecast changes at all.
  2. Monday morning check: Check satellite imagery for the Bay Area — if marine layer is deeper than forecast, it'll be visible. Also pull the updated 12Z winds aloft to confirm the favorable wind picture holds.
  3. File IFR for terrain clearance through the Cascades and Sierra Nevada. Route through established airways or RNAV routing that keeps you clear of terrain — don't scud-run the passes at 12K on a VFR flight plan.
  4. Fuel: KBDN to KSQL is approximately 430 nm. At typical GA cruise speeds, plan 2.5–3 hrs. No weather-related fuel stop needed — just carry legal IFR reserves plus a buffer.
  5. Alternate filed: File KSFO as your alternate. It's close, it's VFR, and it gives you good approach options.
  6. ATIS at KSQL: Get it early on descent — confirm runway in use and wind for crosswind planning.
  7. Departure timing: 21:30Z works well. No reason to push it earlier or later based on current data.

Confidence

What Forecasters Are Saying

  • PDT (Bend departure): High confidence VFR — explicit forecast with light winds through the period. No uncertainty language in the AFD.
  • MTR (Bay Area arrival): High confidence VFR for the afternoon window at SFO/SQL. Morning stratus is the concern, but forecasters expect it to clear well before your arrival. Eddy in Monterey Bay flagged as a wildcard for morning MVFR — not relevant to your afternoon arrival.
  • Terrain effects: Forecasters note moderate westerly winds building into the Bay Area afternoon — sea breeze driven. Expected to stay below 20 kts at SFO. No terrain amplification concern flagged.
  • Trigger condition: If the Monterey Bay eddy is stronger or more persistent than forecast, coastal MVFR could linger longer — but this would need to be badly wrong to affect a late-afternoon arrival.

Overall Confidence: HIGH

The synoptic pattern is locked in — a strong ridge over the West, active storm system confined to the Northeast. There's very little in the forecast that could flip this to a no-go. The only genuine uncertainty is Bay Area marine layer timing, and even a pessimistic outcome puts full clearing well before your arrival. Charts agree, AFDs agree, no terrain surprises indicated. Plan the flight, fly the plan.
